channel.title: Internet on ҜŁΔΜΔŦĦ Ŧ€ĆĦ ĆØŁŁ€ĆŦƗV€
channel.description: Recent content in Internet on ҜŁΔΜΔŦĦ Ŧ€ĆĦ ĆØŁŁ€ĆŦƗV€

item.title: A Website to End All Websites
item.author: contact@klamathtech.diy (ҜŁΔΜΔŦĦ Ŧ€ĆĦ ĆØŁŁ€ĆŦƗV€)
item.description: A personal essay/manifesto arguing that the internet has been industrialized and monopolized by corporate platforms that exploit users rather than serve them — and that the answer is building hand-coded personal websites using open protocols like RSS and ActivityPub. Draws on Ivan Illich’s concept of ‘convivial tools’ that enhance human creativity rather than capture it.

item.title: IndieWeb — Own Your Data
item.author: contact@klamathtech.diy (ҜŁΔΜΔŦĦ Ŧ€ĆĦ ĆØŁŁ€ĆŦƗV€)
item.description: A community and movement built around the principle that people should own their own data and content — publishing on their own domains, using open standards to connect and communicate, rather than depending on corporate platforms that can disappear or change the rules without notice. Practical guides, standards, and community resources.

item.title: What is a Feminist Server?
item.author: contact@klamathtech.diy (ҜŁΔΜΔŦĦ Ŧ€ĆĦ ĆØŁŁ€ĆŦƗV€)
item.description: What would it mean to build digital infrastructure that embodies feminist values — not just in content policy but in governance, ownership, and who the infrastructure serves? Examines feminist server collectives as an alternative model to corporate platforms and explores what accountable, community-governed technology looks like in practice.

item.title: How Did Free Software Build a Social Movement?
item.author: contact@klamathtech.diy (ҜŁΔΜΔŦĦ Ŧ€ĆĦ ĆØŁŁ€ĆŦƗV€)
item.description: A history of how the free and open-source software movement grew from technical practice into political organizing — the values, the licenses, the communities, and the fights over what ‘free’ actually means. Useful context for understanding why software freedom and community autonomy are connected questions.
